did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) optimized by six metaheuristic algorithms (Cuckoo Search, Firefly Algorithm, Whale Optimization Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, Grey Wolf Optimizer, and Crow Search Algorithm) for melanoma detection. The study evaluates these AI models on the HAM10000 dermatological dataset, achieving accuracies up to 91.25%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically a comparative analysis of six metaheuristic algorithms (Cuckoo Search, Firefly Algorithm, Whale Optimization Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, Grey Wolf Optimizer, and Crow Search Algorithm) for optimizing CNN architectures on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section, experimental schemes, performance metrics (Accuracy, Loss, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, AUC), statistical analysis (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, effect sizes, confidence intervals), and new findings regarding model performance, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Analysis of hybrid CNN models optimized with metaheuristic algorithms for melanoma detection
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for accurate melanoma detection by proposing a hybrid optimization framework that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks with six nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithms: Cuckoo Search, Firefly Algorithm, Whale Optimization Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, Grey Wolf Optimizer, and Crow Search Algorithm. Unlike traditional methods that tune fixed architectures, this approach uses population-based optimization to search the architectural and training space for high-performing CNN configurations. The models were evaluated on the HAM10000 dermatological dataset, incorporating a robust preprocessing and data augmentation pipeline to enhance generalization. Experimental results demonstrated that the metaheuristic-optimized CNNs outperformed the baseline, achieving accuracies up to 91.25%. The findings confirm that population-based optimization is an efficient and reliable mechanism for guiding CNN architecture design, offering superior performance compared to traditional manual or other optimization-based strategies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for detection. The study constructs a balanced binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using 5,205 melanoma images (original and augmented) from the HAM10000 dataset.

Used as the negative class in the binary classification task. 5,205 non-melanoma images were randomly selected from remaining lesion categories in the HAM10000 dataset to balance the class distribution. Specific benign lesions mentioned in error analysis include atypical (dysplastic) nevi and benign nevi.

Listed in Table 5 as a cancer type in the ISIC 2018 dataset (7,737 samples). Mentioned in error analysis as a source of false negatives (classified as melanoma) and false positives (benign nevi mimicking melanoma). Not explicitly used as a distinct multi-class label in the final binary experiment.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study constructs a balanced binary classification task with 5,205 melanoma images and 5,205 non-melanoma images. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, and AUC, with confusion matrices provided for class prediction (Class 0: Non-Melanoma, Class 1: Melanoma).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dermatological dataset from the ISIC 2018 challenge containing 11,720 dermoscopic RGB images (600x450 pixels) collected from patients and validated by dermatology specialists. The study utilized a balanced subset of this dataset for a binary melanoma detection task.

use.training: Used for model learning; consisted of 1,305 original melanoma images plus 3,900 augmented images, and 5,205 randomly selected non-melanoma images from other ISIC 2018 categories.
use.validation: Used for evaluating hyperparameter configurations during the metaheuristic optimization process.
use.testing: Used for final performance assessment of the selected model on unseen data.
use.other: The original source contained 11,720 images across 7 diagnostic categories (Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, Actinic Keratosis), but only Melanoma and a balanced selection of Non-Melanoma classes were used in the final experiment.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess whether observed differences in classification performance (Accuracy, Recall, AUC) among metaheuristic algorithms were statistically meaningful.
To control for multiple comparisons during pairwise statistical testing (adjusted alpha = 0.0033).
To quantify the magnitude and direction of pairwise differences (effect size) across experimental schemes.
To estimate 95% confidence intervals for performance metrics (e.g., AUC) to support findings of robustness and consistency.
To summarize the central tendency and variability of performance metrics across the four experimental schemes for each metaheuristic.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in classification performance (Accuracy, Recall, AUC) between the compared metaheuristic algorithms.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in classification performance between the compared metaheuristic algorithms.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study applied a Bonferroni correction to the standard alpha of 0.05 to control for multiple comparisons, resulting in an adjusted alpha threshold of approximately 0.0033 (0.05/15). Pairwise comparisons were conducted, and none of the comparisons reached statistical significance under this adjusted threshold (e.g., CS vs GWO had p ≈ 0.625). The authors note that formal hypothesis testing was inherently low-powered due to the limited number of experimental schemes (n=4) and emphasize effect sizes and confidence intervals as more informative indicators of practical superiority.

HAM10000.notes: The best overall accuracy achieved was 91.25% by Cuckoo Search (CS) in Scheme 1. GWO achieved the highest Specificity (0.9193) in Scheme 2. The baseline CNN-BASE1 achieved an accuracy of 83.69%. Statistical comparisons between metaheuristics showed no significant differences under Bonferroni-corrected Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (alpha = 0.0033).

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) optimized by metaheuristic algorithms (Cuckoo Search, Firefly Algorithm, Whale Optimization Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, Grey Wolf Optimizer, Crow Search Algorithm)

architecture_details.backbone_type: Custom CNN with repeating blocks of 3x3 convolutional filters (padding=1), ReLU activation, and 2x2 max-pooling
architecture_details.baseline_architecture: 3 convolutional layers, 3x3 kernels, 64 or 128 filters, with or without Batch Normalization

training_details.optimizer: Adam (baseline); Optimized learning rates for metaheuristic models
training_details.learning_rate: Baseline: 0.02; Optimized range: ~0.0001 (e.g., 0.000137 for CS, 0.000101 for FA)
training_details.batch_size: Baseline: 128; Optimized range: 16 to 128 (e.g., 63 for CS, 16 for FA)
training_details.epochs: 50 (baseline); Optimized range: 20 to 40 (e.g., 30 for CS, 38 for FA)
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24GB) and RTX 4070 Ti SUPER (16GB)

classification_or_output_head.loss_function: Cross-Entropy Loss (implied by Adam optimizer and standard CNN training context, though not explicitly named in text)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Hybrid CNN models optimized with metaheuristic algorithms
preprocessing_techniques: Image Scaling: Resizing all images to 224 x 224 pixels
Brightness Adjustment: Normalizing illumination by calculating brightness factor as 128 / average grayscale intensity
Hair Removal: Removing artifacts using Top-hat filter or bilateral filtering with morphological opening (kernel 17 x 17)
Random Flip: Horizontal and vertical flip applied randomly with 50% probability
Random Crop: Shear transformation using random factor in range [-0.2, 0.2] in vertical/horizontal direction
Zoom: Zoom factor sampled from range [0.8, 1.2]; images cropped or padded to maintain original size
Rotation: Random rotation within fixed range (e.g., ±15°)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flip applied randomly with 50% probability.
Shear transformation using a random factor in the range [-0.2, 0.2] in the vertical/horizontal direction.
Zoom factor sampled from the range [0.8, 1.2]; images were cropped or padded to maintain original size.
Brightness factor calculated as 128 divided by the average grayscale intensity of each image, clipped to avoid overcorrection.
Random rotation within a fixed range (e.g., ±15°) to introduce angular variability.
Hair removed using Top-hat filter or bilateral filtering with morphological opening (kernel 17 x 17).

conclusions: Metaheuristic-optimized CNNs outperform baseline models, achieving a maximum accuracy of 91.25% on the HAM10000 dataset.

Population-based optimization is an efficient and reliable mechanism for guiding CNN architecture design, offering superior performance compared to traditional manual or other optimization-based strategies.

The Cuckoo Search (CS) algorithm achieved the highest overall performance, with an accuracy of 91.25%, AUC of 0.9120, and Recall of 0.9120, demonstrating high predictive capacity and stable convergence.
The Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) closely followed CS, achieving the highest Specificity (0.9193) and maintaining strong values across other metrics, confirming its robustness.
CS and GWO demonstrated consistent superiority and reliability for CNN optimization in melanoma classification, as supported by bootstrap confidence intervals and effect size analysis.

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) exhibited a tendency to converge to suboptimal local minima with low variability, while the Firefly Algorithm (FA) showed unstable search behavior sensitive to initialization.

Formal hypothesis testing using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test with Bonferroni correction did not reach statistical significance due to the limited number of experimental schemes (n=4); therefore, effect sizes and confidence intervals were emphasized as more informative indicators of practical superiority.
The study acknowledges that detecting small effects would require a larger number of independent experimental schemes, which is a limitation of the current work.

The proposed hybrid optimization framework allows for systematic cross-algorithm comparison under identical conditions, providing insights into convergence stability, exploration-exploitation dynamics, and generalization behavior.

CS and GWO are identified as potentially the most stable and high-performing metaheuristics for CNN tuning in clinical decision-support systems, particularly where minimizing false negatives is critical.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000), which may introduce population bias and limit generalizability to diverse skin types, demographics, or imaging conditions; external validation on heterogeneous datasets (e.g., ISIC 2020, PH2, Derm7pt) is identified as a critical future need.

Formal hypothesis testing using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test with Bonferroni correction (alpha = 0.0033) did not yield statistically significant differences between algorithms due to the low number of experimental schemes (n=4), making effect sizes and confidence intervals the primary indicators of performance.

The best-performing model achieved an accuracy of 91.25%, which is competitive with but slightly lower than the 91.70% reported by Zafar et al. (2023) using DenseNet-201 + Slime Mould Algorithm on the same dataset, though direct comparison is confounded by differences in preprocessing and evaluation protocols.

Error analysis reveals that false negatives are primarily caused by lesions with low chromatic contrast, diffuse boundaries, and residual hair artifacts, while false positives are often atypical (dysplastic) nevi that mimic melanoma features, highlighting intrinsic visual ambiguities in dermoscopic images.

The study acknowledges that the tool must be used strictly as a decision-support system to augment dermatological expertise, not to replace it, due to the severe clinical consequences of false negatives (missed melanoma diagnoses).

Deployment considerations include significant data privacy concerns; the authors suggest future implementations should utilize privacy-preserving technologies such as on-premise processing or federated learning.

The computational cost is high, with GPU execution times for metaheuristic optimization ranging from approximately 10 hours to over 165 hours per algorithm configuration, which may hinder practical clinical adoption without efficient hardware or parallelization.

The dataset was artificially balanced by randomly selecting 5,205 non-melanoma images from various benign categories to match the 5,205 melanoma images, which does not reflect the natural prevalence of lesions in clinical practice.
